title: Warehouse Ventilation Rate
summary: Standard 62.1 recommends 0.3 L/(s·m²) of ventilation as a design criterion for warehouse ventilation.

structured_payload.parameter_name: Minimum outdoor air ventilation rate for general warehouses

evidence_quote: Table 6-1 of Standard 62.1 recommends 0.3 L/(s·m²) of ventilation as a design criterion for warehouse ventilation, although this amount may be insufficient when stored materials have harmful emissions.

title: Warehouse Humidity Control in Humid Climates
summary: In humid climates, infiltration of moist air can cause unacceptably high space humidity when cooling equipment is off due to low sensible load.
evidence_quote: In humid climates, it is common to satisfy the space temperature (i.e., very low or no sensible cooling load), but, because of infiltration of moist air and without proper cooling (i.e., the cooling equipment is off), for space humidity to be unacceptably high.

title: Fume Hood Type for Perchloric Acid
summary: Perchloric acid hoods require special construction with internal washdown systems and materials resistant to acid attack due to explosion hazard.
evidence_quote: Perchloric acid exhaust systems with longer duct runs may need a zoned washdown system to avoid water flow rates in excess of the capacity to drain water from the hood.

title: Cultural Heritage Zoning
summary: Activities posing potential threats to collections (e.g., food areas, loading docks) should be kept away from collections zones, with exhaust located away from fresh air intakes.
evidence_quote: Activities that pose a potential threat to collections environments (food areas, loading docks, fabrication and rough shops, maintenance and housekeeping, etc.) should be kept away from collections zones, with exhaust from these areas located away and downwind from any fresh air intakes for collections systems and the rest of the building.

title: Loading Dock Pressurization
summary: Loading docks should ideally be positively pressurized to mitigate energy transfer and reduce risk of condensation or other issues with shared walls.
evidence_quote: Loading docks should ideally be positively pressurized, and shared walls with collections zones should be carefully designed to mitigate any potential energy transfer, whether from a load perspective or to reduce risk of condensation or other issues.

title: Vapor Pressure Differential and Diffusion
summary: Vapor diffusion occurs from higher to lower vapor pressure and can cause structural damage depending on climate and interior conditions.
evidence_quote: The differential between interior and exterior vapor pressures also drives diffusion (movement of water vapor through permeable materials) from areas of higher vapor pressure to lower.

title: Condensation Risks on Windows/Doors
summary: Windows, doors, and skylights can pose risks as potential condensation points and points of heat transfer, leading to mold, rot, or masonry damage.
evidence_quote: Windows, doors, and skylights can pose risks as potential condensation points, as well as points of heat transfer. Repetitive condensation on doors and windows places that element at risk, and the potential transfer of that moisture to the wall or floor can lead to issues ranging from mold and wood rot to cracking and masonry damage because of freeze/thaw.

title: Latent Capacity in Cultural Heritage Systems
summary: Adequate latent capacity is often disregarded with two-pipe and standard package unit designs, causing relative humidity to rise beyond safe limits as exterior temperatures climb.
evidence_quote: Adequate latent capacity is often disregarded with two-pipe and standard package unit designs, resulting in environments that may be able to maintain temperature control, but commonly see relative humidity conditions rise beyond safe limits as exterior temperatures climb and cooling capacity is dedicated to the sensible load.

title: Occupancy Density for Gallery Design
summary: Some gallery occupancies are as high as 1 m² per person, but unoccupied storage zones may have 100 m² per person or less.
evidence_quote: Some gallery occupancies are as high as 1 m² per person, but unoccupied storage zones may have 100 m² per person or less.

title: Air Curtain Effectiveness Range
summary: Research shows air curtains have a range of effectiveness from 60 to 90%, depending on type and application.
evidence_quote: Research (e.g., Pappas and Tassou [2003]) shows that air curtains have a range of effectiveness from 60 to 90%, depending on the type and application.

title: Air Curtain Minimum Velocity
summary: To maximize effectiveness, the air curtain unit must cover the opening and have a minimum velocity projection of 2 m/s at the target surface.
evidence_quote: To maximize effectiveness, the air curtain unit must at least cover or slightly overlap the entire opening, and have a minimum velocity projection of 2 m/s at the target surface (Wang and Zhong 2014).

title: Non-Recirculating vs. Recirculating Air Curtains
summary: Non-recirculating air curtain units are less expensive and have few installation limitations, while recirculating systems have higher effectiveness but are more expensive and require careful integration.
evidence_quote: Non-recirculating air curtain units comprise the majority of systems built today. There are few limitations to where they can be installed, they have little effect on the construction or design of the building opening, and they are less expensive to install and maintain. ... Recirculating air curtain systems have a higher effectiveness and are more expensive than non-recirculating systems.
